text: Hello, I'm sharing an RTAP for a right inguinal lipoma is what I'll say. You can see there's no peritoneal defect there. This patient has been extremely symptomatic for about two years. Young man has had CAT scans that have not shown a hernia. He came to me with classic symptoms of a hernia. where he felt a reproducible bulge and significant discomfort with activity. I sent him for a dynamic ultrasound which clearly showed that there was fatty tissue sliding in and out of the inguinal canal. And for that reason, I booked him for the surgery that you're seeing here.

Just an unended video, a very thin patient, very easy case, but just highlighting how even without a peritoneal sac and an indirect hernia or a direct hernia, you can have a lipoma and they can often be extremely symptomatic and it's definitely one of the reasons that of the Ten Commandments we should always search for a lipoma even after we have reduced very obvious hernias.
The surgeon carefully maintains a preperitoneal approach, closely following the peritoneum to separate it from the myopectineal orifice, ensuring no indirect sac is present.
I have this at two and a half times speed. Console time was pretty short. This was quite an easy case.

But again, just highlighting how the patient can be extremely symptomatic and we should definitely believe that there is something there. I'm sure many of you would just take the patient to the operating room based upon the symptoms that they had. To be perfectly honest, I could not really appreciate anything on physical exam in the office, despite this being a very thin individual. So I felt that the ultrasound was very helpful in making a diagnosis.

The surgeon continues to dissect the myopectineal orifice (MPO), performing lateral dissection. The chord structures are clearly visible as the surgeon parietalizes them, lifting the peritoneum away and repeating the process towards the vas deferens.

The surgeon prepares to place a twelve by sixteen Progrip mesh, ensuring the pocket is large enough to accommodate it. The patient's small frame and normal-sized internal ring are noted, with minimal peritoneal defect visible.
The surgeon searches for the lipoma, initially unimpressed by the findings. As dissection continues, the surgeon sweeps the cord structures aside and identifies the lipoma.
The surgeon identifies attachments to the nodal tissue posteriorly and performs hemostasis to control any bleeding from the dissection.

The surgeon confirms there is no further fatty tissue entering the canal and ensures hemostasis. The nerves are visible, and the pocket is checked to be large enough for the mesh to unfold completely without clamshelling or folding. The mesh is then placed into the surgical site.

The surgeon observes the flap extending well beyond the mesh placement area, ensuring that the peritoneum cannot slip underneath. The flap is kept straight without folds, and the surgeon prepares to close it.

I think that these cases, particularly when you know there's no other indirect erect femoral hernia on pre-op imaging, would have been more than appropriate to do an open repair in this case with some anterior mesh because you'd easily be able to get the lipoma out from there as well. I just think that avoiding the groin incision in my humble experience has led to decreased pain in my patients and that's why I selected for a minimally invasive approach.

The surgeon identifies a small rent in the tissue and uses a V-lock suture to close it. A fourteen gauge angioclath is placed into the flap to evacuate gas, ensuring the mesh remains opposed to the abdominal wall during desufflation.
